question: What type of number system is binary?
choice_a: A base-10 positional system using digits 0 through 9
choice_b: A base-2 positional counting system using only 0 and 1
choice_c: A base-16 system used to represent hexadecimal values
choice_d: A base-8 system used in legacy computing environments
explanation: Binary is a base-2 positional counting system where each place value is a power of 2 (1; 2; 4; 8; 16...) and only the digits 0 and 1 are used.
aspect_key: base-2 positional counting with digits 0 and 1
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 12 - What Is Binary/Transcript.md

question: Why do computers use binary rather than decimal?
choice_a: Binary calculations are faster in software than decimal
choice_b: Binary was required by early government computing standards
choice_c: Binary maps directly to transistor on/off states making it natural for hardware
choice_d: Binary is easier for humans to read and understand
explanation: Computers are built from transistors — tiny switches that are either on or off. These two physical states map perfectly to binary 1 and 0.
aspect_key: transistor on/off states map to binary 1 and 0
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 12 - What Is Binary/Transcript.md

question: What is a bit?
choice_a: A unit of data storage equal to 1000 bytes
choice_b: A single binary digit with a value of either 0 or 1
choice_c: An 8-character grouping of binary values
choice_d: A multiplier used in IPv4 subnet calculations
explanation: A bit (binary digit) is the smallest unit of data in a computer. It holds exactly one value: 0 or 1.
aspect_key: bit is a single binary digit with value 0 or 1
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: How many bits make up one byte?
explanation: A byte is exactly 8 bits. With 8 bits; the maximum value is 255 and there are 256 total distinct values (2^8).
aspect_key: byte equals 8 bits with range 0 to 255
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: What decimal value does the binary number 10000000 represent?
explanation: 10000000 has a 1 only in the 2^7 position. 2^7 = 128. All other bits are 0 so no other values are added.
aspect_key: add place values only where bits are 1
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 14 - How to convert binary to decimal/Transcript.md

question: What decimal value does uppercase A have in ASCII encoding?
explanation: In ASCII; uppercase A is assigned decimal value 65; which in binary is 01000001. Lowercase a is 97.
aspect_key: uppercase A maps to decimal 65 in ascii
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 12 - What Is Binary/Transcript.md

question: What is a VLAN?
choice_a: A protocol that assigns IP addresses automatically to switch ports
choice_b: A physical cable standard for connecting switches in a data center
choice_c: A routing protocol used to forward traffic between different IP networks
choice_d: A logical grouping of switch ports that creates a separate broadcast domain regardless of physical location
explanation: VLANs provide logical separation on managed switch hardware. Ports grouped into a VLAN share a broadcast domain but are isolated from other VLANs at Layer 2.
aspect_key: logical segmentation on shared switch hardware
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: Which IEEE standard governs VLAN frame tagging on trunk links?
explanation: IEEE 802.1Q (dot1q) is the standard that defines VLAN tagging. It inserts a VLAN tag into Ethernet frames on trunk links so receiving switches know which VLAN each frame belongs to.
aspect_key: ieee 802.1q dot1q governs vlan frame tagging
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: What type of switch port connects an endpoint device such as a PC or printer?
choice_a: Trunk port
choice_b: Uplink port
choice_c: Mirror port
choice_d: Access port
explanation: An access port connects endpoint devices and carries only one untagged VLAN. The device receives frames without any 802.1Q tag attached.
aspect_key: access port connects endpoints and carries one vlan untagged
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: What is the primary purpose of Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)?
choice_a: To assign IP addresses to switches automatically
choice_b: To encrypt traffic between VLAN segments
choice_c: To provide redundant default gateways for end hosts
choice_d: To prevent Layer 2 loops that would cause broadcast storms on switched networks
explanation: Redundant physical links between switches can create Layer 2 loops. STP prevents loops by selecting one active path and blocking redundant alternatives until a failure requires them.
aspect_key: prevents layer 2 loops caused by redundant switch links
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What is the decimal equivalent of binary 10110110?
aspect_key: add place values only where bits are 1
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 14 - How to convert binary to decimal/Transcript.md

question: How many possible VLAN IDs does the 12-bit VLAN ID field in 802.1Q support?
explanation: 12 bits can represent 2^12 = 4096 values. VLAN IDs range from 0 to 4095; giving 4096 possible values in total.
aspect_key: 12-bit vlan id field supports 4096 values from 0 to 4095
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: What is the default behavior of the native VLAN on a Cisco 802.1Q trunk?
choice_a: Native VLAN frames are dropped because they have no tag
choice_b: Native VLAN frames are double-tagged for security
choice_c: Native VLAN is always encrypted on trunk ports
choice_d: Native VLAN frames are sent untagged across the trunk
explanation: By default on Cisco switches; VLAN 1 is the native VLAN. Frames belonging to the native VLAN are sent across trunk links without an 802.1Q tag. This can be changed for security reasons.
aspect_key: native vlan frames transmitted untagged on 802.1q trunk
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: A network switch supports three distinct methods of assigning devices to VLANs. Which set correctly names all three?
choice_a: Access-based; trunk-based; and hybrid VLAN
choice_b: Static; dynamic; and encrypted VLAN
choice_c: Layer 2; Layer 3; and routed VLAN
choice_d: Port-based; tag-based; and protocol-based VLAN
explanation: The three VLAN types are: port-based (VLAN membership tied to the physical port number); tag-based/802.1Q (VLAN ID embedded in the Ethernet frame header enabling trunks); and protocol-based (membership determined by the Layer 3 protocol type of the frame).
aspect_key: port-based; tag-based 802.1q; and protocol-based vlan types
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What is a BPDU and what role does it play in STP?
choice_a: A broadcast packet used by DHCP servers to assign IPs to switches
choice_b: A type of Ethernet frame used to carry VLAN tags across trunk links
choice_c: A keepalive message sent by routers to confirm BGP peer availability
choice_d: A protocol data unit exchanged between switches to elect the root bridge and compute loop-free paths
explanation: BPDUs (Bridge Protocol Data Units) are messages switches exchange to run STP. They carry information for root bridge election; best path computation; and topology change notification (TCN/TCA).
aspect_key: bpdu supports root election and loop-free path computation
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: How much maximum power per port does standard PoE (IEEE 802.3af) deliver?
explanation: IEEE 802.3af (standard PoE) delivers up to 15.4W per port. PoE+ (802.3at) increases this to 30W; and PoE++ (802.3bt) goes to 60W or 100W depending on type.
aspect_key: 802.3af delivers up to 15.4w per port
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What does the show vlan brief command display on a Cisco switch?
choice_a: The current OSPF routing table entries for all VLANs
choice_b: The trunk links and their associated 802.1Q tags
choice_c: The ARP table mapping IP addresses to MAC addresses
choice_d: The list of configured VLANs their names and the ports assigned to each VLAN
explanation: show vlan brief gives a quick summary view: VLAN ID; name; status; and which ports are members. It is the standard verification command after VLAN configuration.
aspect_key: show vlan brief lists vlan ids names and port assignments
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What happens at Layer 2 when a host in VLAN 10 tries to reach a host in VLAN 20 on the same switch?
choice_a: The switch floods the frame to all ports to find the destination
choice_b: The switch forwards the frame because both devices are on the same physical hardware
choice_c: The switch applies NAT to translate between the two VLANs automatically
choice_d: Traffic cannot pass at Layer 2 and a Layer 3 device such as a router is required to route between VLANs
explanation: VLANs are separate Layer 2 broadcast domains. A switch only forwards frames within the same VLAN. Hosts in different VLANs must be routed by a Layer 3 device such as a router or Layer 3 switch.
aspect_key: inter-vlan communication requires layer 3 routing
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: What is the correct Cisco IOS command sequence to assign GigabitEthernet1/0/1 as an access port in VLAN 1?
choice_a: start>show running-config>enable vlan 1
choice_b: enable>configure terminal>interface Gi1/0/1>switchport access vlan 1
choice_c: enable>configure terminal>interface 1>switchport mode access>switchport access vlan 1>exit>exit
choice_d: enable>configure terminal>interface Gi1/0/1>switchport mode access>switchport access vlan 1>exit>exit
explanation: To assign an interface as an access port: enter the interface with 'interface Gi1/0/1'; then set 'switchport mode access'; and finally 'switchport access vlan 1'. The mode command must come first.
aspect_key: switchport mode access then switchport access vlan assignment
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: What is the SolarWinds TFTP Server primarily used for in network administration?
choice_a: Assigning IP addresses to Cisco routers via BOOTP
choice_b: Providing encrypted file transfer for switch IOS upgrades
choice_c: Monitoring VLAN traffic between managed switches
choice_d: Backing up Cisco router and switch configurations by receiving copied files over the network
explanation: The SolarWinds TFTP Server is a free tool used to receive router and switch configuration backups. A router uses the copy command to send its running config to the TFTP server IP where it is saved as a named file.
aspect_key: solarwinds tftp receives cisco device configuration backups
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 15 - SolarWinds TFTP Server/Transcript.md

question: What is the decimal value of binary 101?
explanation: Binary 101 = (1x4) + (0x2) + (1x1) = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5. Only add place values where the bit is 1.
aspect_key: add place values only where bits are 1
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 14 - How to convert binary to decimal/Transcript.md

question: Why is leaving switch ports in auto trunk negotiation mode a security risk?
choice_a: Auto negotiation disables 802.1Q tagging causing frame loss on trunk links
choice_b: Auto negotiation broadcasts VLAN IDs in cleartext enabling passive eavesdropping
choice_c: Trunk negotiation causes STP topology changes that expose routing tables
choice_d: An attacker can connect a rogue switch which negotiates a trunk and gains access to traffic across multiple VLANs
explanation: If a switch port is left in auto/dynamic mode; a rogue switch can trigger trunk negotiation. The attacker's device then receives tagged frames from all VLANs on that trunk — a VLAN hopping risk. Best practice is to force ports to access mode explicitly.
aspect_key: auto trunk negotiation enables vlan hopping via rogue switch
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: In a router-on-a-stick design what is the role of each router subinterface on the physical trunk link to the switch?
choice_a: Each router subinterface is typically assigned to one VLAN processes frames for that VLAN's 802.1Q tag and often serves as that VLAN's Layer 3 default gateway
choice_b: The entire physical router interface represents only one VLAN gateway so subinterfaces are optional labels with no routing role
choice_c: Each router subinterface replaces the switch access port for one individual host
choice_d: Each router subinterface disables VLAN tagging so the switch can route between VLANs by itself
explanation: In router-on-a-stick one physical router interface operates as a trunk and can carry traffic for multiple VLANs. The router creates separate subinterfaces and each subinterface is usually associated with one VLAN by encapsulation dot1Q. That subinterface often holds the default-gateway IP address for hosts in that VLAN. The physical interface carries many VLANs while each subinterface usually maps to one VLAN.
aspect_key: each router subinterface usually maps to one vlan on the trunk
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What is the key advantage of RSTP over classic STP?
choice_a: RSTP supports more than 4096 VLANs while classic STP is limited to 1024
choice_b: RSTP eliminates BPDUs and uses flooding to detect topology changes
choice_c: RSTP assigns root bridges by MAC address only while classic STP uses bridge priority
choice_d: RSTP achieves significantly faster convergence often under 10 seconds compared to the much longer timers of classic STP
explanation: Classic STP convergence can take 30-50 seconds due to listening and learning timer delays. RSTP (Rapid STP) redesigns the state machine and negotiation process to converge in seconds improving network recovery time significantly.
aspect_key: rstp achieves faster convergence than classic stp
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What criterion determines which switch wins the root bridge election in STP?
choice_a: The switch with the most active ports is elected root
choice_b: The switch with the highest MAC address is always elected root
choice_c: Root election is random and re-runs after every topology change
choice_d: The switch with the lowest bridge ID which combines bridge priority and MAC address wins the root election
explanation: Bridge ID = bridge priority (default 32768) + MAC address. The switch with the numerically lowest bridge ID becomes root. Admins can lower priority manually to control which switch wins the election.
aspect_key: lowest bridge id wins root election combining priority and mac
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: Why does 802.1Q tagging on trunk links add overhead and what networking concern does this create?
choice_a: Tagging adds a second IP header increasing CPU processing at every router hop
choice_b: Tagging replaces the EtherType field preventing normal IPv4 packet forwarding
choice_c: The tag doubles the FCS field causing CRC errors on segments without jumbo frame support
choice_d: The VLAN tag increases the Ethernet frame size which can cause fragmentation or drops on devices that enforce strict standard MTU limits
explanation: 802.1Q inserts a 4-byte tag into Ethernet frames. This grows frame size beyond the standard 1500-byte payload MTU. Devices that enforce strict MTU limits may drop or fragment these larger tagged frames. MTU consistency across the path must be planned and verified.
aspect_key: 802.1q tag increases frame size risking drops on strict mtu devices
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: In binary place-value notation why does adding one more bit to a number dramatically expand the maximum representable value?
choice_a: Each additional bit adds a fixed value of 256 to the maximum
choice_b: Additional bits only increase range if the leftmost bit is set to 1
choice_c: More bits increase resolution not maximum value by dividing the same range more finely
choice_d: Each additional bit doubles the maximum representable range because each new place value is exactly twice the previous highest value
explanation: Binary is base-2 positional. Each bit position is worth double the position to its right (1; 2; 4; 8; 16...). Adding one bit adds a new place value equal to all previous bits combined plus one — so the total range doubles. 8 bits max 255; 9 bits max 511; 16 bits max 65535.
aspect_key: each additional bit doubles maximum representable range
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: What is a TCP MSS value of 1460 designed to achieve in standard IP/Ethernet environments?
choice_a: It limits the number of VLANs that can be carried on a single trunk to 1460
choice_b: It sets the maximum number of TCP segments per second the switch can process
choice_c: It defines the maximum distance in meters for 802.1Q trunk links
choice_d: It sizes TCP payload to fit within a standard 1500-byte Ethernet MTU after subtracting 20-byte IP header and 20-byte TCP header to avoid fragmentation
explanation: Standard Ethernet MTU is 1500 bytes. IPv4 header = 20 bytes; TCP header = 20 bytes. That leaves 1460 bytes for TCP payload. Setting MSS to 1460 ensures TCP segments fit inside one Ethernet frame without triggering IP fragmentation.
aspect_key: tcp mss 1460 fits 1500-byte mtu minus 20-byte ip and tcp headers
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: What is ASCII?
choice_a: A wireless networking standard for connecting devices to access points
choice_b: A Cisco proprietary protocol for assigning switch port security policies
choice_c: A character encoding standard introduced in 1963 that maps numbers to letters and symbols
choice_d: A binary arithmetic method for performing addition and subtraction
explanation: ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange) was introduced in 1963. It uses 7 bits to provide 128 values covering uppercase and lowercase letters; digits 0-9; punctuation; and control codes.
aspect_key: ascii maps numbers to characters using 7-bit standard
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: What problem did Unicode solve that ASCII could not?
choice_a: Unicode replaced binary with hexadecimal for more efficient data storage
choice_b: Unicode added encryption to text data preventing eavesdropping on shared networks
choice_c: ASCII values 128-255 were used differently by different systems causing garbled text; Unicode provides a single universal standard for all languages and symbols
choice_d: Unicode replaced ASCII by reducing character encoding from 7 bits to 4 bits
explanation: Extended ASCII (values 128-255) was defined differently by different countries and vendors. Files crossing incompatible systems displayed garbled characters called mojibake. Unicode (introduced 1992) resolves this with a single universal encoding covering all world languages; symbols; and emoji.
aspect_key: unicode resolves extended ascii incompatibility across systems
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: What data size unit is equal to exactly 8 bits?
choice_a: Nibble
choice_b: Kilobyte
choice_c: Word
choice_d: Byte
explanation: A byte is exactly 8 bits. It can represent 256 distinct values (2^8) ranging from 0 to 255. Data size prefixes such as kilobyte; megabyte; and gigabyte are all multiples of bytes.
aspect_key: byte equals exactly 8 bits
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: What does PoE stand for and what does it deliver?
choice_a: Protocol over Ethernet — a method for encapsulating multiple protocols on one link
choice_b: Power over Ethernet — delivers both data and electrical power over a standard Ethernet cable
choice_c: Port on Ethernet — identifies which switch port is active on a managed switch
choice_d: Packet over Ethernet — a tunneling method for forwarding Layer 3 packets across Layer 2
explanation: PoE (Power over Ethernet) delivers both data and electrical power over a standard Ethernet cable. This eliminates separate power supplies for powered devices such as IP phones; wireless access points; and IP cameras.
aspect_key: poe delivers data and power over ethernet cable
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: Which Cisco IOS command saves the running configuration to NVRAM so it survives a reboot?
choice_a: write startup
choice_b: reload save
choice_c: copy startup-config running-config
choice_d: copy running-config startup-config
explanation: copy running-config startup-config saves the active configuration from RAM to NVRAM. The write memory command does the same thing. Without saving; any configuration changes are lost when the switch reboots.
aspect_key: copy running-config startup-config saves config to nvram
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What is a PD in a PoE network?
choice_a: A Packet Distributor that routes frames between VLANs
choice_b: A Protocol Descriptor field in an 802.1Q VLAN tag
choice_c: A Policy Domain used in QoS traffic shaping on managed switches
choice_d: A Powered Device — any endpoint that receives electrical power over the Ethernet cable from a PSE
explanation: PD stands for Powered Device. It is any device that receives power from the Ethernet cable. Common examples include IP phones; wireless access points; and IP cameras. The PSE (Power Sourcing Equipment) provides the power.
aspect_key: pd is powered device receiving power over ethernet
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: Which Cisco IOS command displays a summary of all interfaces with their IP address and up/down status?
choice_a: show version
choice_b: show ip interface brief
choice_c: show vlan brief
choice_d: show mac address-table
explanation: show ip interface brief provides a compact table of all interfaces showing IP address; line status; and protocol status. It is useful for quickly identifying which interfaces are up or down.
aspect_key: show ip interface brief displays interface ip and status summary
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What does the MOTD banner do on a Cisco switch?
choice_a: It reboots the switch on a scheduled timer and logs the event
choice_b: It displays a message to anyone connecting to the device before the login prompt
choice_c: It sets the switch hostname shown in routing table advertisements
choice_d: It enables SNMP monitoring for the management interface
explanation: The MOTD (Message of the Day) banner is configured with banner motd in global config. It is displayed before the login prompt on any connection method and is typically used for legal warning notices about unauthorized access.
aspect_key: motd banner displays message to users before login prompt
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: On a new or factory-reset Cisco switch what VLAN are all ports in by default?
choice_a: VLAN 99
choice_b: VLAN 1
choice_c: VLAN 100
choice_d: VLAN 0
explanation: A factory-new or reset switch places all ports in VLAN 1 by default. All hosts connected to that switch can initially communicate with each other. VLANs must be explicitly configured to create isolation between groups.
aspect_key: all ports default to vlan 1 on a new or reset switch
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: What is the maximum decimal value an 8-bit binary number can represent?
explanation: An 8-bit number has place values 128; 64; 32; 16; 8; 4; 2; 1. When all 8 bits are 1 the sum is 255. There are 256 total distinct values (0 through 255) because 2^8 = 256.
aspect_key: 8-bit maximum value is 255 with 256 distinct values
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: What does VTP stand for and what is its purpose?
choice_a: VLAN Transfer Protocol — moves endpoint devices between VLANs automatically
choice_b: VLAN Table Protocol — synchronizes MAC address tables across all switches in a network
choice_c: VLAN Trunking Protocol — Cisco proprietary protocol that propagates VLAN definitions from a server switch to client switches
choice_d: Virtual Terminal Protocol — provides remote CLI access to Cisco switches over a serial link
explanation: VTP (VLAN Trunking Protocol) is Cisco proprietary. It propagates VLAN definitions from a VTP server switch to VTP client switches within the same VTP domain. This reduces the need to manually configure VLANs on every switch.
aspect_key: vtp propagates vlan definitions from server to client switches
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What command on a Cisco switch displays IOS version hardware model uptime and last reboot reason?
choice_a: show interfaces status
choice_b: show ip interface brief
choice_c: show running-config
choice_d: show version
explanation: show version displays the IOS software version; hardware model; switch uptime; and the reason for the last reboot. Frequent unexpected reboots visible here may indicate hardware or software problems.
aspect_key: show version displays ios version hardware uptime and reboot reason
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What decimal value does uppercase A have in ASCII?
explanation: In the ASCII encoding standard uppercase A is assigned decimal value 65. Lowercase a is 97. These values are standardized so any system using ASCII will interpret byte value 65 as the letter A.
aspect_key: uppercase A equals decimal 65 in ascii
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 12 - What Is Binary/Transcript.md

question: Which IOS mode do you enter with the enable command?
choice_a: Interface configuration mode
choice_b: Line configuration mode
choice_c: User EXEC mode
choice_d: Privileged EXEC mode
explanation: The enable command transitions from user EXEC mode (Switch>) to privileged EXEC mode (Switch#). From privileged EXEC you can access configure terminal to enter global configuration mode.
aspect_key: enable command enters privileged exec mode
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: Which IOS command sets a descriptive name for a Cisco switch that appears in the CLI prompt?
choice_a: enable secret
choice_b: banner motd
choice_c: hostname
choice_d: ip address
explanation: The hostname command in global configuration mode sets the device name. The CLI prompt immediately changes to reflect the new hostname. It also identifies the device in logs and neighbor tables.
aspect_key: hostname command sets device name shown in cli prompt
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What is a benefit of dividing a network into multiple VLANs?
choice_a: VLANs increase physical cable speed by reducing electrical resistance
choice_b: VLANs allow all switch ports to share a single MAC address table entry
choice_c: VLANs automatically encrypt traffic between departments
choice_d: VLANs reduce broadcast domain size improving performance and providing security isolation between departments
explanation: Key VLAN benefits include smaller broadcast domains (broadcasts only reach devices in the same VLAN); security isolation between departments or groups; and flexible logical topology without physical rewiring.
aspect_key: vlans reduce broadcast domains and provide security isolation
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: What command on a Cisco switch shows learned MAC addresses and which ports they were seen on?
choice_a: show interfaces trunk
choice_b: show ip arp
choice_c: show running-config
choice_d: show mac address-table
explanation: show mac address-table displays the MAC address learning table mapping each learned MAC address to the VLAN and port where it was seen. This is useful for locating specific devices and verifying that frames are being forwarded correctly.
aspect_key: show mac address-table maps mac addresses to switch ports
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What is the first step in switch troubleshooting before using any CLI command?
choice_a: Check the STP topology for blocked ports
choice_b: Run show running-config to review configuration
choice_c: Reload the switch to clear any temporary states
choice_d: Check physical cables link LEDs patch panel connections and device power
explanation: Physical-first troubleshooting means verifying physical layer items first: cables are plugged in securely; link LEDs show connectivity; patch panel connections are correct; and the device has power. Many problems are physical and do not require CLI investigation.
aspect_key: physical layer checks precede cli troubleshooting
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What does ISL stand for and what replaced it?
choice_a: Inter-Switch Load-balancing — replaced by LACP for link aggregation
choice_b: Integrated Switch LAN — replaced by 802.3af for PoE delivery
choice_c: Inter-Switch Link — a Cisco proprietary VLAN trunking protocol replaced by the industry-standard 802.1Q
choice_d: Internal Spanning-tree Link — a loop-prevention method replaced by RSTP
explanation: ISL (Inter-Switch Link) was a Cisco proprietary trunking protocol that encapsulated the entire Ethernet frame. It has been replaced by the IEEE 802.1Q standard which inserts a 4-byte tag inside the frame. ISL is now obsolete.
aspect_key: isl is obsolete cisco proprietary trunking replaced by 802.1q
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: What is the purpose of the enable secret command on a Cisco switch?
choice_a: It sets the password required to access the console port via a local cable
choice_b: It encrypts all traffic sent over VTY lines to prevent eavesdropping
choice_c: It assigns an IP address to the management VLAN interface
choice_d: It sets a hashed password that protects privileged EXEC mode from unauthorized access
explanation: enable secret stores an MD5-hashed password protecting the privileged EXEC mode prompt (Switch#). It is always preferred over the older enable password command which uses weak reversible encoding.
aspect_key: enable secret sets hashed password protecting privileged exec mode
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What is an SVI on a Cisco switch?
choice_a: A Spanning-tree Violation Indicator triggered when a BPDU is received on an access port
choice_b: A Secure VLAN Interface that encrypts traffic between VLANs on the same switch
choice_c: A physical uplink port that connects two switches for inter-VLAN routing
choice_d: A Switched Virtual Interface — a virtual Layer 3 interface used to assign a management IP address to a switch
explanation: An SVI (Switched Virtual Interface) is a virtual Layer 3 interface created on a Layer 2 switch with the interface vlan command. On a pure Layer 2 switch it is used only to assign a management IP address for remote access; not for routing between VLANs.
aspect_key: svi is virtual layer 3 interface for switch management ip
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What is the TFTP backup purpose on a Cisco network?
choice_a: TFTP backs up switch firmware and operating system images to the cloud
choice_b: TFTP provides encrypted remote access to Cisco devices over the internet
choice_c: TFTP transfers running configurations from Cisco routers and switches to a backup server for recovery
choice_d: TFTP monitors bandwidth utilization and stores statistics on a management server
explanation: TFTP (Trivial File Transfer Protocol) is used to copy running configurations from Cisco devices to a TFTP server. This provides a backup copy that can be used to restore the device if the configuration is lost or the device needs to be replaced.
aspect_key: tftp copies running configs to backup server for recovery
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 15 - SolarWinds TFTP Server/Transcript.md

question: What does the show interfaces status command display on a Cisco switch?
choice_a: Only interfaces that are in an error-disabled state
choice_b: A list of VLAN trunk links and their allowed VLAN IDs
choice_c: Port status; VLAN assignment; duplex; and speed for all switch interfaces
choice_d: The STP state (forwarding or blocking) for each port
explanation: show interfaces status gives a quick port-by-port overview showing whether each port is connected; the VLAN it is assigned to; its duplex setting; and its speed. It is commonly used to check for wrong VLAN assignments.
aspect_key: show interfaces status shows port vlan duplex and speed
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What does the binary number system use as its base?
choice_a: Base 8
choice_b: Base 16
choice_c: Base 10
choice_d: Base 2
explanation: Binary is a base-2 number system. It uses only the digits 0 and 1. Each place value is a power of 2 (1; 2; 4; 8; 16...) compared to decimal which is base-10 with place values of powers of 10.
aspect_key: binary is base-2 using only digits 0 and 1
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 12 - What Is Binary/Transcript.md

question: Which STP port state forwards normal user traffic and learns MAC addresses?
choice_a: Blocking
choice_b: Listening
choice_c: Learning
choice_d: Forwarding
explanation: The forwarding state is the normal active operating state where a port sends and receives user traffic and learns MAC addresses. It also sends and receives BPDUs. All other STP states before forwarding restrict data traffic.
aspect_key: forwarding state is the active state that passes user traffic
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What is the console line security command sequence to require a password for local console access?
choice_a: enable console 0 then password then exit
choice_b: interface console 0 then security password
choice_c: line vty 0 then password then login
choice_d: line console 0 then password then login
explanation: To secure the console port: enter line console 0; then set the password with password <pw>; then enable login to require it. Without the login command the password is not enforced.
aspect_key: line console 0 then password then login secures console access
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: How many bits does the IEEE 802.1Q VLAN ID field use?
choice_a: 4 bits supporting 16 values
choice_b: 8 bits supporting 256 values
choice_c: 16 bits supporting 65536 values
choice_d: 12 bits supporting 4096 values
explanation: The 802.1Q VLAN tag includes a 12-bit VLAN ID field. 2^12 = 4096 possible values. VLAN IDs range from 0 to 4095; with VLAN 0 and 4095 reserved and VLAN 1 as the default.
aspect_key: 12-bit vlan id field in 802.1q supports 4096 values
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: What does LACP do in a switched network?
choice_a: LACP is a Cisco proprietary STP variant that runs per VLAN
choice_b: LACP is a protocol for assigning VLAN IDs to trunk links automatically
choice_c: LACP translates between ISL and 802.1Q trunking on mixed-vendor networks
choice_d: LACP bundles multiple physical links into one logical port-channel providing both additional bandwidth and redundancy
explanation: LACP (Link Aggregation Control Protocol) is IEEE 802.3ad. It combines multiple physical Ethernet links into a single logical link called a port-channel. Member ports must match speed and duplex. This increases throughput and provides redundancy if a link fails.
aspect_key: lacp bundles physical links into one logical port-channel
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What Cisco IOS command creates a new VLAN and enters VLAN configuration mode?
choice_a: create vlan <id>
choice_b: vlan database add <id>
choice_c: interface vlan <id>
choice_d: vlan <id>
explanation: Entering vlan <id> in global configuration mode creates the VLAN and places you in VLAN configuration mode (Switch(config-vlan)#). From there you can assign a name with the name command.
aspect_key: vlan id command creates vlan and enters vlan config mode
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What is the voice VLAN command used for on a switch port?
choice_a: It creates a dedicated VLAN for management traffic on the switch CPU
choice_b: It forces the port into trunk mode to carry all VLANs for the IP phone
choice_c: It places the entire port into a voice-only VLAN so the PC cannot access the network
choice_d: It allows an IP phone to tag its voice traffic in a separate VLAN while data traffic from an attached PC stays in the untagged data VLAN
explanation: switchport voice vlan <id> lets a port handle both an IP phone and a downstream PC. The phone tags its own voice traffic with the specified VLAN ID while the PC data traffic remains untagged in the access VLAN.
aspect_key: switchport voice vlan lets ip phone tag voice in separate vlan
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What does a PSE do in a PoE deployment?
choice_a: PSE is the endpoint device that receives power over the Ethernet cable
choice_b: PSE is the VLAN that carries PoE management traffic between switches
choice_c: PSE provides electrical power on the Ethernet cable to supply power to connected PDs
choice_d: PSE is the protocol that negotiates bandwidth between PoE switch ports
explanation: PSE (Power Sourcing Equipment) is the device that puts power onto the Ethernet cable. A PoE switch port is an endpoint PSE. An external power injector added between a non-PoE switch and a PD is a midspan PSE.
aspect_key: pse provides power on ethernet cable to connected pds
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What is the purpose of the show power inline command?
choice_a: It displays VLAN membership and 802.1Q tag state for PoE-enabled ports
choice_b: It shows the spanning-tree state of ports connected to powered devices
choice_c: It shows per-port PoE power usage and the overall PoE budget for the switch
choice_d: It displays the PoE firmware version and discovery protocol in use
explanation: show power inline displays the total PoE power budget available; how much is being used; and per-port power status and consumption. It is the primary tool for troubleshooting PoE device power failures.
aspect_key: show power inline displays poe budget and per-port power status
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What does the STP blocking state do?
choice_a: It forwards normal user traffic at reduced speed while waiting for a superior BPDU
choice_b: It disables the port entirely so no frames of any kind are processed
choice_c: It floods all traffic out all ports to discover MAC addresses before forwarding
choice_d: It does not forward data traffic but still receives and processes BPDUs to participate in loop prevention
explanation: A port in the blocking state does not forward user data frames but still receives BPDUs. This allows it to participate in the STP topology so it can transition to forwarding if a better path fails.
aspect_key: blocking state receives bpdus but does not forward user traffic
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What is the VTY line used for on a Cisco switch?
choice_a: VTY lines connect to peripheral hardware like console cables and USB adapters
choice_b: VTY lines provide power management for PoE-enabled switch ports
choice_c: VTY lines carry VLAN trunk traffic between distribution switches
choice_d: VTY lines provide remote Telnet and SSH access to manage the switch over the network
explanation: VTY (Virtual Teletype) lines on Cisco IOS handle remote connections. line vty 0 15 configures all 16 VTY lines. Adding transport input ssh restricts access to SSH only eliminating less secure Telnet access.
aspect_key: vty lines provide telnet and ssh remote access to the switch
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What binary sequence immediately follows 0111 when counting in binary?
explanation: In binary counting 0111 represents decimal 7. Adding 1 causes a carry to cascade: 0111 + 1 = 1000 which is decimal 8. This demonstrates the carry rule where adding 1 to all-1s resets the lower bits and creates a new leading bit.
aspect_key: binary carry produces 1000 after 0111
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 12 - What Is Binary/Transcript.md

question: What is the main reason VLANs are preferred over buying a separate switch for each department?
choice_a: Separate switches are physically larger and require more power than VLANs
choice_b: VLANs provide built-in encryption while separate switches transmit in cleartext
choice_c: A dedicated switch per department is more expensive; less flexible; and less scalable than logical VLAN separation on shared managed switch hardware
choice_d: Separate switches cannot use 802.1Q trunking so they cannot support multiple uplinks
explanation: Physical separation using a dedicated switch per department does work but it is more costly (additional hardware); less flexible (physical moves required for reorganization); and less scalable. VLANs achieve the same isolation logically on shared hardware.
aspect_key: vlans are more flexible and cheaper than physical separation
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: What do the STP listening and learning states have in common?
choice_a: Both forward user traffic while waiting for BPDU confirmation
choice_b: Both prevent MAC address learning to protect against loops
choice_c: Both are disabled when RSTP is enabled on a switch
choice_d: Both are transitional states that do not forward user data traffic
explanation: During the listening state STP determines the topology (sends and receives BPDUs) but does not forward data and does not learn MACs. During the learning state it begins learning MACs but still does not forward data. Both are transitional — the port is working toward forwarding.
aspect_key: listening and learning are transitional states without data forwarding
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: Which PoE standard delivers up to 30W per port?
choice_a: 802.3bt Type 4
choice_b: 802.3bt Type 3
explanation: IEEE 802.3at (PoE+) delivers up to 30W per port. Standard 802.3af delivers up to 15.4W. PoE++ (802.3bt) Type 3 delivers up to 60W and Type 4 up to 100W.
aspect_key: 802.3at poe plus delivers up to 30w per port
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What is Cisco PVST+?
choice_a: A proprietary extension of 802.11 wireless for VLAN-aware access points
choice_b: A per-VLAN spanning tree implementation compatible with 802.1Q where each VLAN runs its own STP instance
choice_c: A PoE power negotiation protocol used on Cisco catalyst switches
choice_d: A trunk negotiation protocol that replaced ISL on 802.1Q trunk links
explanation: Cisco PVST+ (Per-VLAN Spanning Tree Plus) runs a separate STP instance for each VLAN. This allows per-VLAN root bridge selection and enables load balancing across redundant links. PVST+ is the 802.1Q-compatible version of Cisco's per-VLAN STP.
aspect_key: pvst plus runs a separate stp instance per vlan compatible with 802.1q
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What does a duplex mismatch between two directly connected switch ports cause?
choice_a: The port with the lower duplex setting automatically negotiates to match the other
choice_b: A duplex mismatch forces both ends to half-duplex as a safety measure
choice_c: Duplex mismatch is harmless and only affects port speed not error counters
choice_d: Late collisions; input errors; and significantly degraded throughput on the link
explanation: When one end of a link is full-duplex and the other is half-duplex the half-duplex side uses CSMA/CD and generates late collisions when the full-duplex side sends without checking. This causes input errors and drops visible in show interface counters.
aspect_key: duplex mismatch causes late collisions and input errors
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What is the decimal equivalent of binary 11001010?
explanation: 11001010 = (1x128) + (1x64) + (0x32) + (0x16) + (1x8) + (0x4) + (1x2) + (0x1) = 128 + 64 + 8 + 2 = 202. This is the same value used in the Video 13 binary addition example.
aspect_key: add place values only where bits are 1
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: A 32-bit unsigned integer can store a maximum of approximately how many values?
choice_a: About 65000
choice_b: About 2.1 billion
choice_c: About 256
choice_d: About 4.3 billion
explanation: 2^32 = 4294967296 which is approximately 4.3 billion distinct values for unsigned 32-bit integers. Signed 32-bit integers split the range roughly ±2.1 billion by reserving one bit for the sign.
aspect_key: 32-bit unsigned maximum is approximately 4.3 billion
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: Why does a 32-bit operating system have an addressable RAM limit of approximately 4 GB?
choice_a: The OS kernel uses half of RAM for system processes leaving only half for user programs
choice_b: 32-bit systems use hexadecimal addressing which caps out at 4 GB by definition
choice_c: 4 GB is a hardware enforced limit set by BIOS regardless of OS bit-width
choice_d: A 32-bit address bus can reference at most 2^32 unique byte addresses which equals approximately 4 GB of memory
explanation: Memory addresses in a 32-bit system are 32-bit values. 2^32 = 4294967296 unique addresses. Since each address typically refers to one byte the maximum addressable RAM is ~4 GB. 64-bit systems extend this to terabytes.
aspect_key: 32-bit address space limits addressable ram to approximately 4 gb
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: How does IEEE 754 store non-integer floating-point values in 32-bit format?
choice_a: Using two separate 16-bit integers where one holds the whole part and one holds the decimal part
choice_b: Using a 32-bit integer with an implicit decimal point always fixed at bit 16
choice_c: By storing values as ASCII text codes that the CPU converts on each calculation
choice_d: With 1 sign bit; 8 exponent bits; and 23 significand bits structured like scientific notation
explanation: IEEE 754 32-bit float: 1 bit for sign; 8 bits for exponent; 23 bits for significand. This is like scientific notation (e.g. 625.9 = 0.6259 x 10^3). The floating decimal point can represent a wide range of magnitudes.
aspect_key: ieee 754 uses sign exponent and significand to store floats
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: Why do kilobyte definitions sometimes differ between 1000 and 1024 bytes depending on context?
choice_a: The difference was resolved in 2000 and all modern systems now use 1024 exclusively
choice_b: Kilobyte is only defined as 1024 bytes; the 1000-byte value is a marketing error with no standard basis
choice_c: Storage manufacturers use 1000 while OS memory tools use 1024 because of the way binary addressing works in powers of 2
choice_d: The international standard now mandates 1024 for all uses; 1000 is used only in data transmission speeds
explanation: SI (decimal) prefixes define 1 KB = 1000 bytes. Binary (IEC) defines 1 KiB = 1024 bytes (2^10). Storage manufacturers often use 1000-based values making drives appear larger while operating systems use 1024-based values resulting in the apparent size difference.
aspect_key: kb can mean 1000 or 1024 bytes depending on decimal or binary context
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: Why did ASCII succeed as an interoperability standard for early computer systems?
choice_a: ASCII was mandatory under U.S. federal law for all computing equipment sold after 1960
choice_b: ASCII used an 8-bit encoding that was compatible with punch card hardware used at the time
choice_c: ASCII succeeded because it was proprietary to IBM giving it market dominance
choice_d: Different manufacturers adopted a common 7-bit encoding so computers could reliably exchange text regardless of vendor
explanation: Before ASCII different systems used incompatible character codes. ASCII provided a shared standard (128 values using 7 bits) that all systems could implement. This enabled reliable text exchange across different manufacturers — the definition of interoperability.
aspect_key: common ascii standard enabled text exchange across different systems
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: What binary value does the ASCII character lowercase 'a' have given its decimal value of 97?
explanation: Decimal 97 in binary: 64 + 32 + 1 = 97; place values active are 64 (2^6) and 32 (2^5) and 1 (2^0). That gives 01100001. Compare to uppercase A = 65 = 01000001; only bit 5 differs.
aspect_key: lowercase a is decimal 97 equal to binary 01100001
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: What is mojibake and what causes it?
choice_a: A Japanese encoding format used as a replacement for ASCII in East Asian systems
choice_b: Garbled text that occurs when a file encoded in one character set is read by a system using a different incompatible character set
choice_c: A Unicode error that occurs when emoji characters exceed the 3-byte UTF-8 encoding limit
choice_d: A type of binary overflow that happens when an ASCII value exceeds decimal 127
explanation: Mojibake is the garbled or nonsensical characters produced when text is interpreted using the wrong character encoding. It commonly occurred when files created on systems using one extended-ASCII variant were opened on systems with a different variant for values 128-255.
aspect_key: mojibake is garbled text from incompatible character encoding
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: Which command correctly sequences the steps to assign interface fa0/5 to VLAN 20 as an access port?
choice_a: conf t > interface fa0/5 > switchport access vlan 20 > switchport mode access
choice_b: conf t > switchport mode access fa0/5 > switchport access vlan 20
choice_c: conf t > vlan 20 > interface fa0/5 > no shutdown
choice_d: conf t > interface fa0/5 > switchport mode access > switchport access vlan 20
explanation: The switchport mode access command must be issued before switchport access vlan. Entering mode first explicitly sets the port as an access port; then the VLAN assignment is applied. Reversing the order can cause unexpected behavior on some platforms.
aspect_key: switchport mode access must precede switchport access vlan assignment
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: What is the role of Francis Bacon's 5-bit encoding in the history of computer character encoding?
choice_a: 5-bit encoding became the basis of ASCII and is still used in modern character sets
choice_b: Bacon's encoding demonstrated that as early as the 1600s binary sequences could represent alphabet characters; providing historical context for why computers use binary for text
choice_c: Bacon created the first 7-bit encoding which was later standardized into ASCII in 1963
choice_d: 5-bit encoding was used for the first telegraph transmissions in Morse code
explanation: In the 1600s Francis Bacon used 5-bit sequences to encode letters. 5 bits allow only 32 values — barely enough for a basic alphabet. This illustrates the historical pattern: more bits are needed as character sets grow. ASCII used 7 bits; Unicode now covers hundreds of thousands of characters.
aspect_key: bacon 5-bit encoding shows historical need for more bits as character sets grow
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: What happens to the 802.1Q VLAN tag when a trunk port delivers a frame to an access port endpoint?
choice_a: The tag is preserved so the endpoint can identify which VLAN it belongs to
choice_b: The tag is converted from 802.1Q to ISL format before delivery to the endpoint
choice_c: The tag is replaced by a VLAN name string in the Ethernet header
choice_d: The switch strips the tag before sending the frame out the access port so the endpoint receives an untagged frame
explanation: Trunk ports carry tagged frames between network devices. When a frame is forwarded out an access port the switch removes the 802.1Q tag. The endpoint device receives a normal untagged Ethernet frame and has no awareness of the VLAN.
aspect_key: switch strips 802.1q tag before sending frame out access port
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: What problem does a mismatched native VLAN between two trunk switches cause?
choice_a: Native VLAN mismatch prevents the trunk from forming so both ports fall back to access mode
choice_b: Frames from the mismatched VLAN are silently forwarded into the wrong VLAN on the receiving switch causing unintended cross-VLAN traffic
choice_c: Native VLAN mismatch only matters when VTP is disabled; VTP corrects it automatically
choice_d: Native VLAN mismatch forces all trunk traffic to be double-tagged increasing frame overhead
explanation: The native VLAN is sent untagged. If SW1 native VLAN is 1 and SW2 native VLAN is 10; frames SW1 sends untagged (thinking they are VLAN 1) will be received by SW2 as VLAN 10. This causes unexpected cross-VLAN traffic and can be exploited in VLAN hopping.
aspect_key: native vlan mismatch forwards untagged frames into wrong vlan
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 7/VLAN - Virtual Local Area Network.md

question: What is the purpose of the encapsulation dot1Q command on a router subinterface?
choice_a: It sets the router subinterface to use ISL encapsulation for backward compatibility
choice_b: It changes the subinterface MTU to account for the 4-byte 802.1Q tag overhead
choice_c: It assigns the subinterface a VLAN ID so it processes frames tagged with that VLAN ID on the trunk link
choice_d: It binds a specific MAC address to the subinterface to prevent IP spoofing on that VLAN
explanation: In a router-on-a-stick setup each subinterface handles one VLAN. The encapsulation dot1Q <vlan-id> command tells the router which VLAN tags to accept and process on that subinterface. It must match the VLAN ID being trunked from the switch.
aspect_key: encapsulation dot1q assigns vlan id to router subinterface
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What does the show interfaces trunk command output tell a network administrator?
choice_a: It shows the root bridge election result and blocked port locations for each VLAN
choice_b: It confirms which ports are currently operating as trunk links; which VLANs are allowed and active; and the encapsulation type
choice_c: It shows which ports are in PoE power fault state and what their last negotiated power level was
choice_d: It displays detailed frame error counters including CRC and runts for all trunk interfaces
explanation: show interfaces trunk lists active trunk ports with their mode; encapsulation type (typically 802.1Q); the VLANs allowed on each trunk; and VLANs that are active and forwarding. It is the primary trunk verification command.
aspect_key: show interfaces trunk shows active trunks allowed vlans and encapsulation
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: How does MSTP differ from Cisco PVST+ in handling VLANs and spanning tree instances?
choice_a: MSTP uses ISL trunking for multi-instance support while PVST+ requires 802.1Q
choice_b: MSTP cannot be used with more than 64 VLANs while PVST+ scales to all 4094 VLANs
choice_c: MSTP runs one spanning tree instance per switch while PVST+ runs one per VLAN
choice_d: MSTP maps multiple VLANs to a smaller number of spanning tree instances enabling load sharing while PVST+ runs a fully separate instance per VLAN
explanation: Cisco PVST+ creates one STP instance per VLAN which scales poorly with many VLANs. MSTP (IEEE 802.1s) lets multiple VLANs share an instance. This reduces CPU overhead while still enabling VLAN-aware load balancing across redundant links.
aspect_key: mstp maps multiple vlans to fewer instances unlike pvst plus one per vlan
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What does the show interface <id> command specifically reveal that show interfaces status does not?
choice_a: It shows VLAN assignment and trunk state for the selected interface
choice_b: It reveals the physical MAC address assigned to the interface
choice_c: It shows detailed error counters such as CRC errors; runts; giants; and collision counts
choice_d: It displays the STP port state and root port status for that interface
explanation: show interfaces status gives a quick summary (state; VLAN; speed; duplex). show interface <id> goes deeper: input/output packet counts; error counters (CRC; runts; giants; collisions); and queue stats. Incrementing error counters indicate physical layer or duplex problems.
aspect_key: show interface id reveals error counters crc runts giants collisions
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What does an up/down interface status indicate on a Cisco switch compared to down/down?
choice_a: Down/down means the port has been manually disabled with the shutdown command; up/down can also mean manual shutdown
choice_b: Up/down and down/down are identical states only displayed differently depending on IOS version
choice_c: Down/down means physical link present but no protocol; up/down means no physical signal detected
choice_d: Up/down means physical connectivity is present but the data-link protocol has not come up; down/down means no physical signal is detected at all
explanation: Interface status has two components: line (physical) and protocol. Up/down = physical carrier detected but Layer 2 protocol is not up (encapsulation mismatch or far-end issue). Down/down = no physical signal. Administratively down = manually shut with the shutdown command.
aspect_key: up/down is physical link no protocol; down/down is no physical signal
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: In a network with redundant links and no loop prevention what specific failure mode would occur?
choice_a: Switches would fail to learn any MAC addresses due to frame collision overload
choice_b: Broadcast frames would circulate indefinitely between switches consuming all bandwidth in a broadcast storm
choice_c: Redundant links would automatically load-balance traffic with no negative effect on the network
choice_d: All switches would independently elect themselves root bridges causing routing failures
explanation: Without STP a broadcast frame entering a loop exits one switch port; travels a redundant link to the next switch; gets forwarded out all ports including back toward the original switch; and the cycle repeats. This broadcast storm escalates exponentially saturating all bandwidth within seconds.
aspect_key: without stp redundant links cause broadcast storms that consume all bandwidth
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What are the three BPDU types and what is each used for?
choice_a: Hello BPDU; Goodbye BPDU; and Reboot BPDU used for switch lifecycle management
choice_b: Configuration BPDU for root election and topology; TCN to signal a topology change; and TCA to acknowledge the TCN
choice_c: Data BPDU for forwarding decisions; Control BPDU for root election; and Error BPDU for fault reporting
choice_d: Root BPDU; Designated BPDU; and Blocked BPDU corresponding to port roles in the STP topology
explanation: The three BPDU types are: (1) Configuration BPDU — carries root bridge ID; sender bridge ID; and path cost information for root election and topology computation; (2) TCN (Topology Change Notification) — signals that the topology has changed; (3) TCA (TCN Acknowledgment) — confirms receipt of a TCN.
aspect_key: configuration bpdu tcn and tca are the three stp bpdu types
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What is the binary result of adding 10110111 and 00010011 and what is the decimal sum?
explanation: 10110111 = 183; 00010011 = 19; 183 + 19 = 202. In binary: 10110111 + 00010011 carries produce 11001010 which equals 128 + 64 + 8 + 2 = 202. This is the worked example from Video 13.
aspect_key: binary addition of 10110111 and 00010011 produces 11001010 equals 202
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List A - Video Content/Video 13 - representing numbers and letters with binary/Transcript.md

question: What is the STP hello timer and what does it control?
choice_a: The hello timer controls how long a port waits in the listening state before transitioning to learning
choice_b: The hello timer is the maximum age a saved BPDU is considered valid before being discarded
choice_c: The hello timer is 15 seconds and defines the forward delay during topology convergence
choice_d: The hello timer is 2 seconds by default and controls how frequently switches send BPDUs to their neighbors
explanation: The STP hello timer defaults to 2 seconds. Every 2 seconds the root bridge sends configuration BPDUs. These BPDUs propagate through the network keeping other switches informed of the topology and allowing them to detect if the root bridge has gone silent.
aspect_key: stp hello timer defaults to 2 seconds controlling bpdu send frequency
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: A technician plugs a PC into a switch port but the PC cannot ping anything in its VLAN. show interfaces status shows the port is connected and the speed and duplex are correct. What is the most likely cause?
choice_a: The switch is out of PoE power and cannot power the PC network adapter
choice_b: The PC NIC is configured for half-duplex while the switch port is full-duplex
choice_c: The port has been assigned to the wrong VLAN
choice_d: STP has permanently blocked the port and it will never transition to forwarding
explanation: When physical connectivity is confirmed (link up; correct speed/duplex) but the device cannot communicate in its VLAN; wrong VLAN assignment is the first thing to check. show interfaces status shows the VLAN; confirm it matches what was intended.
aspect_key: wrong vlan assignment causes connectivity failure with good physical link
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/citavault/NETC-121/Notes List B - Textbook Content/Essential - Chapter 8 Introduction to Switch Configuration/Essential Chapter 8 - Switch Configuration

question: What sequence of Cisco IOS modes must be traversed to configure an interface as an access port on VLAN 10?
choice_a: user EXEC > privileged EXEC > global config > VLAN config > interface config
choice_b: user EXEC only if the port is not already in a VLAN; otherwise privileged EXEC is sufficient
choice_c: privileged EXEC > line config > interface config > VLAN config
choice_d: user EXEC > privileged EXEC > global config > interface config
explanation: Start at user EXEC (Switch>); use enable to reach privileged EXEC (Switch#); then configure terminal to reach global config (Switch(config)#); then interface <id> to reach interface config (Switch(config-if)#). From there issue switchport mode access and switchport access vlan 10.
aspect_key: access port vlan config requires user exec to priv exec to global to interface
